bibliographic_data.title: New Results on Instruction Cache Attacks
bibliographic_data.abstract: We improve instruction cache data analysis techniques with a framework based on vector quantization and hidden Markov models. As a result, we are capable of carrying out efficient automated attacks using live I-cache timing data. Using this analysis technique, we run an I-cache attack on OpenSSL’s DSA implementation and recover keys using lattice methods. Previous I-cache attacks were proof-of-concept: we present results of an actual attack in a real-world setting, proving these attacks to be realistic. We also present general software countermeasures, along with their performance impact, that are not algorithm specific and can be employed at the kernel and/or compiler level.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper improves instruction cache data analysis techniques using vector quantization and hidden Markov models to automate attacks on I-cache timing data, successfully recovering keys from OpenSSL’s DSA implementation.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors use a templating framework that combines Vector Quantization (VQ) and Hidden Markov Models (HMM) to analyze instruction cache timing data. This allows for the automation of side-channel data analysis steps, enabling efficient attacks on cryptographic implementations like OpenSSL’s DSA.
outcomes: Successfully recovered keys from OpenSSL’s DSA implementation using lattice methods.
Presented real-world attack results proving I-cache attacks to be practical and realistic.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Cache-timing attacks are emerging as a significant threat in security-critical software. This paper focuses on instruction cache (I-cache) attacks, which exploit the behavior of modern computer systems at the microarchitectural level. The authors improve existing I-cache data analysis techniques by applying vector quantization and hidden Markov models to automate the side-channel data analysis step. They demonstrate this technique by mounting a lattice attack on an unmodified OpenSSL DSA implementation, successfully recovering keys. This work is significant as it provides real-world results of I-cache attacks, proving their practicality in a real-world setting. Additionally, the paper outlines general software countermeasures that can be implemented at the kernel and/or compiler level to mitigate these attacks.
